coredata.dc:title: Green-emitting carbon dots-protein fluorescence sensing system for specific and sensitive detection of trypsin in urine
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects

Wang, Xiujuan

Wu, Zhiyu
Yang, Jiawei

Wang, Zhibing
Sun, Guoying
coredata.dc:description: Trypsin is a kind of digestive enzyme capable of catalyzing the hydrolysis of proteins. Abnormal changes in the levels of trypsin can cause a series of pancreatic-related diseases. Therefore, based on the special interface interaction between green-emitting ionic liquid-based carbon dots (G-CDs) and BSA, a highly sensitive fluorescence "on-off" mechanism was developed for detecting trypsin in urine. The G-CDs were synthesized by a one-step hydrothermal approach using 2,5-dihydroxybenzoic acid (2,5-DHBA) and 1-octyl-3-methylimidazolium tetrafluoroborate ([OMIM]PF4). The interaction between G-CDs and bovine serum albumin (BSA) led to the formation of the G-CDs-BSA fluorescence sensing system, which induced aggregation and fluorescence enhancement of G-CDs. The G-CDs-BSA system was disrupted under optimal incubation conditions after the addition of trypsin, and the fluorescence was quenched. According to the fluorescence quenching value (∆F) of the system, the quantitative analysis of trypsin in urine was realized and demonstrated good recoveries. This study not only developed a novel fluorescence sensing system with excellent performance for trypsin detection, but also provided a theoretical basis for the binding of carbon dot-protein systems.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Ionic liquid
Fluorescence sensing system
Trypsin

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM and HR-TEM image of G-CDs. (B) Diameter distribution of G-CDs. (C) FT-IR spectra of G-CDs. (D) XPS spectrum of the G-CDs. The XPS analysis of (E) C 1 s, (F) N 1 s, (G) O 1 s, (H) B 1 s, and (I) F 1 s.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra of the G-CDs. (B) Examination of the optimal excitation wavelength of G-CDs. Effects of (C) pH, (D) NaCl, (E) UV irradiation time, and (F) temperature on G-CDs.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of the "on-off" detection mechanism. (B) Fluorescence spectra of BSA after adding different concentrations of G-CDs. (C) Fluorescence lifetimes of G-CDs and G-CDs+BSA. (D) S-V curves of G-CDs on BSA. (E) UV–vis absorption spectra of G-CDs, BSA, and G-CDs+BSA. (F) The variation curve of lg (F0-F)/F with lg [G-CDs]. (G) fluorescence emission spectrum of BSA and UV–vis absorption spectrum of G-CDs. (H) Circular dichroism of BSA and BSA+G-CDs. (I) Scanning electron microscopy images of G-CDs in the presence of BSA.

The effects of (A) BSA concentration, (B) pH, (C) incubation time, and (D) incubation temperature on the ∆F of G-CDs-BSA system. (E) Effects of different interfering substances on ∆F. (F) The relationship between ∆F and trypsin concentration [C] (10−6-10−1 g/mL).
The detection process of fluorescence sensing system.

head.ce:title: Green-emitting carbon dots-protein fluorescence sensing system for specific and sensitive detection of trypsin in urine

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Life Science, Changchun University of Technology, 2055 Yanan Street, Changchun 130012, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Life Science, Changchun University of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Advanced Institute of Materials Science, Changchun University of Technology, 2055 Yanan Street, Changchun 130012, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Advanced Institute of Materials Science, Changchun University of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Advanced Institute of Materials Science, Changchun University of Technology, 2055 Yanan Street, Changchun 130012, P R China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Chemistry and Life Science, Changchun University of Technology, 2055 Yanan Street, Changchun 130012, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Life Science, Changchun University of Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Trypsin is a kind of digestive enzyme capable of catalyzing the hydrolysis of proteins. Abnormal changes in the levels of trypsin can cause a series of pancreatic-related diseases. Therefore, based on the special interface interaction between green-emitting ionic liquid-based carbon dots (G-CDs) and BSA, a highly sensitive fluorescence "on-off" mechanism was developed for detecting trypsin in urine. The G-CDs were synthesized by a one-step hydrothermal approach using 2,5-dihydroxybenzoic acid (2,5-DHBA) and 1-octyl-3-methylimidazolium tetrafluoroborate ([OMIM]PF4). The interaction between G-CDs and bovine serum albumin (BSA) led to the formation of the G-CDs-BSA fluorescence sensing system, which induced aggregation and fluorescence enhancement of G-CDs. The G-CDs-BSA system was disrupted under optimal incubation conditions after the addition of trypsin, and the fluorescence was quenched. According to the fluorescence quenching value (∆F) of the system, the quantitative analysis of trypsin in urine was realized and demonstrated good recoveries. This study not only developed a novel fluorescence sensing system with excellent performance for trypsin detection, but also provided a theoretical basis for the binding of carbon dot-protein systems.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Trypsin is a kind of protein enzyme belonging to the digestive enzyme category that can catalyze the hydrolysis reaction of proteins and break them down into amino acids and small peptides [1]. It is primarily secreted by the pancreas and enters the small intestine, where it collaborates with other pancreatic enzymes to participate in the digestion process of food [2]. This process allows the body to absorb and utilize the protein from food and provides the necessary nourishment to various tissues and organs [3]. When there are abnormal changes in the concentration levels of trypsin, it may lead to a series of pancreatic-related diseases. In the absence of sufficient trypsin, the protein in the food cannot be fully digested, which results in poor nutrient absorption and indigestion [4]. Conversely, excessive activation of trypsin can damage pancreatic tissues, trigger inflammation and pain, and elevate the trypsin levels in the urine [5]. Regular monitoring of trypsin concentration in urine not only helps to early screen for pancreatic-related diseases but also facilitates assessing the treatment efficacy of the diseases. It provides valuable information for physicians to improve the health status of patients [6,7]. Therefore, rapid and accurate detection of trypsin levels in urine is crucial for the diagnosis and treatment of pancreatic-related diseases.

Conventional methods for trypsin detection include the gelatin film method, enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA), mass spectrometry, electrochemical analysis, gel electrophoresis, and fluorescence analysis [8]. Although these methods can successfully determine trypsin concentration and exhibit some advantages, there are also some limitations. For example, the gelatin film method detects the activity of trypsin by observing the clarity of the hydrolysis area [9]. Although this method is simple, convenient, and cost-effective, it lacks precision in enzyme activity measurement. Mass spectrometry determines the presence and quantity of trypsin on the basis of its molecular weight and fragment mass-to-charge ratio [10]. It demonstrates high sensitivity and accurate quantification of trypsin. However, the required equipment is too expensive, and the analysis time is too long. ELISA method employs enzyme-labeled antibodies for the specific recognition and quantification of trypsin [11]. It exhibits high specificity and fast detection speed, which is suitable for high-throughput sample analysis, but its poor stability and the need for suitable assay kits limit its applications. Electrochemical analysis can realize on-line monitoring by using electrochemical sensors to detect the change in electrochemical signal caused by trypsin [12,13]. However, it heavily relies on environmental conditions and electrode surface properties. Fluorescence analysis detects trypsin by measuring the change in fluorescence intensity caused by its interaction with fluorescence sensors [14,15]. It is worth noting that this method possesses high sensitivity, rapid detection, and broad applicability to different sample types, which make it highly promising to detect trypsin [16,17]. But it is susceptible to the activity of trypsin and the optical properties of samples. Therefore, the selection of suitable fluorescence sensors plays a decisive role in the fluorescence analysis of trypsin.

Carbon dots (CDs) are a kind of nano-sized particle mainly composed of carbon elements with diameters ranging lower than 10 nm. They exhibit excellent optical properties, good stability, and strong adaptability to changes in environmental and biological systems [18,19]. These characteristics make them play a crucial role in fluorescence imaging, optoelectronic devices, and biosensing [20,21]. Consequently, CDs as fluorescence sensors for the analysis of complex biological systems has received more and more attention. However, there were only a few reports regarding CDs-based trypsin detection, and the CDs in these studies exhibited poor optical properties. Some additional nanomaterials were required to assist trypsin detection [22,23]. This not only added to the material preparation steps but also increased detection costs. Among various types of CDs, IL-CDs inherit the properties of both ionic liquids and CDs. The structure and properties of IL-CDs can be accurately controlled by adjusting the composition of the ionic liquid and reaction conditions. For example, ionic liquids with carboxyl groups can enhance the hydrophilicity and negative charge of CDs, stabilizing their dispersion in water. When reaction time was prolonged, the surface of CDs was further oxidized, which can improve the fluorescence intensity and stability of the CDs [24]. Importantly, IL-CDs possess low toxicity, low volatility, and good biocompatibility, which make them highly suitable for applications in the field of biosensing [25]. Based on the above advantages, in this study, IL-CDs were selected as fluorescent sensors for the detection of trypsin in urine.

At present, most of the fluorescence analyses based on CDs directly detect the target through fluorescence signals [26]. Although direct fluorescence detection requires no additional steps, it is highly susceptible to interference from other factors, such as background fluorescence, light source intensity, and the degree of probe-target binding. In particular, it is not suitable for fluorescence direct detection when the target substance concentration is low or the sample matrix is complex. Therefore, a fluorescence "on-off" mechanism based on the principle of aggregation-induced fluorescence enhancement was developed for the determination of trypsin content in urine ( Scheme 1). Initially, G-CDs were synthesized using [OMIM]PF4 and 2,5-dihydroxybenzoic acid (2,5-DHBA) as precursors. The interaction between G-CDs and BSA induced the aggregation and fluorescence enhancement of G-CDs. After the addition of trypsin, the structure of BSA was disrupted and G-CDs re-dispersed in the solution, leading to a decrease in fluorescence intensity. Rapid and accurate detection of trypsin was achieved by the fluorescence quenching value (∆F) of G-CDs-BSA system. This study not only realized the high specificity and sensitivity of the detection of trypsin in urine but also provided a new approach for the fluorescence detection method of protease.

Pepsin (Pep), amylase (Amy), lipase (Lip), and trypsin (Try) were obtained from Yuanye Biotechnology Co., Ltd. [OMIM]PF4, 2,5-DHBA, and other chemical reagents (Cu2 +, Ag+, Mn2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Zn2+, Mg2+, Al3+, K+, Na+, Ca2+) were supplied by Aladdin Co., Ltd. Ultra-pure water was provided by the HHitech Master Touch-S15UVF laboratory water purification system.

The fluorescence spectra were collected on Horiba-FluroroMax-Plus fluorescence spectrometer. UV–vis absorption spectra were measured by Shimadzu-UVmini-1280. FT-IR spectra were collected for surface functional groups on Thermofisher-Nicolet-IS50 infrared spectrometer. The morphology and high-resolution images were obtained by JEOL-JEM-2100F transmission electron microscopy. Elemental species and contents were collected by Malvern-Panalytical-X‘Pert3 X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy. X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectra were obtained from Rigaku-SmartLab-9kW X-ray diffractometer.

Initially, 0.1 g of 2,5-DHBA and 0.5 g of [OMIM]PF4 were added to a 50 mL reaction kettle. The mixture was dissolved in 20 mL of water and heated at 180°C for 12 h in an oven. The G-CDs solution was subjected to high-speed centrifugation for 10 min after 1 h of cooling. The solution was then dialyzed in water for 24 h using a dialysis bag with a 500 Da molecular weight cutoff. The solution was freeze-dried to collect G-CDs, which were stored at 4°C under dark conditions.

Firstly, G-CDs, BSA, trypsin, and other enzymes were dissolved in deionized water, respectively. Then, 100 μL (12 mg/mL) of G-CDs solution and 100 μL (30 mg/mL) of BSA solution were added to 2.9 mL of buffer solution with pH 8.0. After thorough mixing, a G-CDs-BSA system was formed in the solution. Subsequently, 100 μL (250 U/mg) of different concentrations of trypsin solution were added, and the mixture was incubated at 37°C for 20 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The pretreatment method for real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, the urine samples were placed in a 10 mL centrifuge tube and centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 5 min. Then, the supernatant was collected and filtered through a 0.22 µm membrane to remove suspended particles. Finally, the filtered solution was adjusted to pH 8 and stored at 4°C for further experiments.

The G-CDs exhibited uniformly dispersed spherical morphology with a normal distribution in the range of 1.0–3.5 nm and an average particle size of 2.48 ± 0.62 nm ( Fig. 1A and B). The high-resolution transmission electron microscopy image of G-CDs demonstrated obvious lattice stripes with a 0.18 nm spacing (Fig. 1A and Fig. S1). The XRD spectrum showed a prominent wide peak at 2θ = 20°, which was attributed to the (002) lattice structure (Fig. S2) [27]. As depicted in Fig. 1 C, the peak at 3450 cm⁻¹ was assigned to the O-H stretching vibration, while the peak at 2932 cm⁻¹ corresponded to the C-H stretching vibration [28]. The broad peak at 1660 cm−1 represented the stretching vibration of CO, while the peak at 1079 cm−1 denoted the stretching vibration of C-O [29]. As shown in Fig. 1D, five peaks appeared at 285.9 eV (C, 63.37 %), 401.8 eV (N, 6.73 %), 533.2 eV (O, 10.47 %), 685.8 eV (B, 5.56 %), and 195.0 eV (F, 13.87 %). The C 1 s spectrum exhibited three peaks at 284.7 eV, 286.3 eV, and 289.1 eV, corresponding to C-C, C-N, and C-O bonds, respectively (Fig. 1E) [30]. The N 1 s spectrum demonstrated two peaks at 401.4 eV and 401.9 eV, corresponding to C-N and N-H structures (Fig. 1F) [31]. And the O 1 s spectrum exhibited two peaks, which were assigned to the C-O structure at 532.0 eV and the CO structure at 532.9 eV (Fig. 1G) [32]. The peaks at 193.8 eV and 194.2 eV were observed in the B 1 s spectrum, which represented the B-C and B-O bonds (Fig. 1H) [33]. The semi-ionic and covalent C-F bonds were attributed to the peaks at 685.3 eV and 685.8 eV, respectively (Fig. 1I). The above results indicated that G-CDs were successfully doped with various atoms, which contributed to the further enhancement of their optical properties.

The suitability of G-CDs for trypsin detection is largely determined by their optical properties. The absorption peak of G-CDs at 215 nm was assigned to the π → π* transition of CC bonds ( Fig. 2A) [34]. The presence of non-conjugated chromophores in the G-CDs was indicated by the peak at 288 nm, which corresponded to the n → π* transition [35]. Due to the presence of surface defects, the G-CDs demonstrated significant excitation wavelength dependence in the range of 340 nm to 390 nm (Fig. 2B). As shown in Fig. S3, the fluorescence lifetime of G-CDs was determined to be 4.59 ns with an QY of 13.82 %. The fluorescence stability of G-CDs was investigated to ensure their stable operation in real sample detection. As illustrated in Fig. 2C, the fluorescence intensity of G-CDs decreased by less than 20 % in the pH range of 3.0–10.0. The fluorescence intensity of G-CDs maintained almost unchanged at the optimal pH value of trypsin (pH 8.0). Moreover, the G-CDs maintained high fluorescence intensity in the NaCl concentration range of 10−1 - 10−7 mol/L, which provided support for subsequent experiments (Fig. 2D). Furthermore, the fluorescence intensity of G-CDs remained stable even after 10 min of continuous irradiation (Fig. 2E). Finally, the effects of temperature on the fluorescence intensity of G-CDs were examined, and no change in fluorescence intensity was observed at high temperatures (80°C) (Fig. 2F). The results indicated that the G-CDs had good fluorescence stability, enabling their successful application in various environments for trypsin detection.

In view of the various limitations of fluorescence direct detection of trypsin, an "on-off" fluorescence detection method was developed, and its detection mechanism was thoroughly explored. In Fig. 3A, the fluorescence intensity of G-CDs significantly increased after the addition the BSA and then decreased after the addition of trypsin for 20 min. The fluorescence quenching value (∆F) enabled the quantitative detection of trypsin. This fluorescence phenomenon might arise from the interaction between G-CDs and BSA, which resulted in the formation of G-CDs-BSA system and aggregation-induced fluorescence enhancement. This aggregation structure shortened the distance between G-CDs, reducing non-radiative pathways and allowing more excited-state energy to be released as fluorescence, thereby enhancing fluorescence. The π-π stacking effect from aggregation altered the electronic environment of the carbon dots, raised the excited-state energy level, and caused a blue shift in the emission peak. After the addition of trypsin, the hydrolysis of BSA disrupted the interaction between G-CDs and BSA, which led to a decrease in the fluorescence intensity of the system. Therefore, the interaction between BSA and G-CDs was studied to verify this conjecture.

The subsequent experiments were conducted using BSA as the fluorophore and G-CDs as the quencher. If the quenching mechanism is static, it would indicate the formation of a static complex between BSA and G-CDs, leading to their binding. As the concentration of G-CDs increased, the fluorescence intensity of BSA gradually decreased (Fig. 3B). The quenching of BSA may be ascribed to static quenching resulting from complex formation. It was demonstrated by fluorescence lifetime decay curves, the Stern-Volmer equation, and UV–vis absorption spectra. As exhibited in Fig. 3C, the fluorescence lifetime of G-CDs did not change before or after the addition of BSA. Additionally, the Stern-Volmer equation was utilized to determine the quenching constant (Ksv) of the G-CDs-BSA system [36]:
(1)
F
0
F
= 1 +
K
sv
[ Q ]

F0 and F signified the fluorescence intensities of BSA before and after the addition of CDs, and [Q] denoted the concentration of G-CDs. The three Stern-Volmer curves exhibited good linear relationships (Fig. 3D). As the temperature increased, the S-V fluorescence quenching constant gradually decreased (1.195 ×104, 1.029 ×104, and 1.018 ×104 L/mol). Additionally, the absorption spectra of BSA changed after the addition of G-CDs, which indicated the formation of new complexes between G-CDs and BSA (Fig. 3E). These results suggested that the fluorescence quenching mechanism of BSA by G-CDs was attributed to static quenching. Furthermore, the double logarithm equation was employed to calculate the binding constant (Ka) between G-CDs and BSA [37]:
(2)
lg (
F
0
− F
F
) = lg
K
a
+ nlg [ Q ]

In the equation, F and F0 denoted the fluorescence intensities of BSA before and after the addition of G-CDs, respectively. [Q] represented the concentration of G-CDs in the mixed solution. A strong linear correlation between lg(F0-F)/F and lg[G-CDs] was observed at different temperatures (Fig. 3F). It was found that Ka for the binding of G-CDs to BSA fell in the range of 107 with an approximate n value of 2. This indicated that G-CDs possessed two binding sites on BSA and exhibited strong binding affinity. Moreover, the type of binding force between BSA and G-CDs could be determined by the enthalpy change (∆H) and entropy change (∆S) of the G-CDs-BSA system. ∆H, ∆S, and ∆G could be calculated by the following Van't Hoff equation (Table S1):
(3)
ln
K
a
= − ∆ H / TR + ∆ S / R
(4)
∆ G = ∆ H − T ∆ S

In the formula, Ka represented the binding constants of the system at the respective temperatures, R was the gas constant, and T denoted the thermodynamic temperature. The results indicated that ∆G was negative, which suggested that the binding process between G-CDs and BSA was spontaneous, and hydrogen bonding and van der Waals forces played a primary role. This was attributed to the abundance of oxygen-containing groups on the surface of G-CDs, facilitating the formation of hydrogen bonds with BSA.

As depicted in Fig. 3G, some overlap was observed between the fluorescence emission spectrum of BSA and the absorption spectrum of G-CDs. The binding distance was calculated as r = 3.63 nm (r < 7 nm) by equations (S1), (S2), and (S3). It can be inferred that fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) also occurred between BSA and G-CDs. Furthermore, the circular dichroism spectrum of BSA showed two absorption bands at 210 nm and 218 nm, corresponding to characteristic peaks of protein α-helical structures (Fig. 3H). After the addition of G-CDs, a significant change in the ellipticity of BSA was observed, and the α-helical content of the protein increased from 7.65 % to 12.77 %. It suggested that the interaction between G-CDs and BSA led to a secondary structure change for BSA. Finally, electron microscopy images demonstrated that the average particle size of nanoparticles increased to 1 μm, due to the aggregation of G-CDs induced by their interaction with BSA, which further explained the cause of the fluorescence enhancement phenomenon (Fig. 3I). At this point, the addition of trypsin would disrupt the interaction between BSA and G-CDs, causing the G-CDs to re-disperse and resulting in a decrease in fluorescence intensity (Fig. S4).

To achieve highly sensitive detection of trypsin, the experimental conditions (BSA concentration, pH, incubation temperature, and incubation time) were researched. The fluorescence quenching value (∆F) of the G-CDs-BSA system reached its maximum when the BSA concentration was 30 mg/mL ( Fig. 4A). And there was no significant change in ∆F observed as the BSA concentration increased. Therefore, 30 mg/mL of BSA concentration was chosen for the subsequent experiments. After adding trypsin, the change in ∆F was investigated in the range of pH (7.0–11.0). As exhibited in Fig. 4B, the maximum ∆F occurred at pH 8.0, aligning with the optimal pH for trypsin activity. Consequently, the reaction pH was set at 8.0, and the effect of incubation time on ∆F was examined. ∆F reached its maximum and remained stable after 20 min, which indicated that trypsin had completely hydrolyzed BSA. The incubation time was set to 20 min for subsequent testing (Fig. 4C). Since both high and low temperatures would inhibit the activity of trypsin, 37°C was determined to be the optimal incubation temperature for trypsin detection (Fig. 4D).

The selectivity of the G-CDs-BSA system for trypsin was crucial for sample detection. When different interfering ions (Cu2+, Ag+, Mn2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Zn2+, Mg2+, Al3+, K+, Na+ and Ca2+), interfering compounds (AA, PDA and Glu) and interfering enzymes (Pep, Amy, and Lip) were added to the G-CDs-BSA system, the fluorescence intensity remained relatively unchanged after 20 min of incubation (Fig. 4E). However, ∆F increased significantly after adding trypsin, indicating good selectivity of the G-CDs-BSA system for trypsin detection. Additionally, the sensitivity of the G-CDs-BSA system under optimal incubation conditions was researched. The fluorescence intensity of G-CDs-BSA system decreased with the increase of trypsin concentration (Fig. S5). More importantly, the ∆F of G-CDs-BSA system increased gradually with the increase in trypsin concentration, and a good linear relationship between ∆F of the G-CDs-BSA system and -lg[C] was observed (Fig. 4F). The linear equation was calculated as ∆F = 49.43 + 7.64 lg[C] (R2 = 0.9997) with an LOD of 0.021 μg/mL. ∆F represented the fluorescence quenching value of the G-CDs-BSA system after the addition of trypsin, and [C] denoted the concentration of trypsin. These results suggested that the method could be used for highly selective and sensitive detection of trypsin. Furthermore, the comparison of G-CDs-BSA system with other trypsin fluorescence probes indicated that the G-CDs-BSA system possessed a wider linear range, a lower detection limit, and a faster response time (Table S2). Compared to other CDs-based trypsin detection methods, IL-CDs enabled trypsin detection without additional nanomaterial due to their superior optical properties, which simplified the procedure and reduced the cost.

The detection effect of G-CDs-BSA on trypsin in urine was explored to verify the feasibility of this method in complex matrices. Firstly, the trypsin content in the blank sample and the recoveries of trypsin at three different spiked concentrations were determined, respectively. The spiked experimental results demonstrated that the recoveries of this method ranged from 98.39 % to 102.80 % (Table S3). Three parallel measurements were conducted, and the relative standard deviations (RSD) were below 4.0 %, indicating that the experiment had good reproducibility. A T-test was performed on the positive sample (HPLC and this method). Statistical analysis showed that there was no significant difference between the two methods at the 95 % confidence level (Table S4). The above results indicated that the G-CDs-BSA fluorescent probe could quickly and accurately detect trypsin in urine.

In summary, a novel fluorescent sensor using [OMIM]PF4 and 2,5-DHBA as precursors was synthesized. The interaction between BSA and G-CDs formed a G-CDs-BSA system, leading to aggregation-induced fluorescence enhancement of G-CDs. The G-CDs-BSA system was destroyed after adding trypsin, and the fluorescence intensity decreased. Through this mechanism, high sensitivity and high sensitivity for the detection of trypsin in urine were successfully achieved. Compared with other trypsin fluorescent sensors, G-CDs exhibited a lower LOD and a wider detection range. In this study, a new trypsin detection method based on the CDs-protein binding mechanism was developed, and an effective tool for rapid diagnosis of pancreatic diseases was provided.

Chenzhao Wang: Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Conceptualization. Jiawei Yang: Validation, Methodology. Zhiyu Wu: Validation, Methodology. Yuhao Jiang: Validation, Methodology. Xiujuan Wang: Validation, Methodology. Guoying Sun: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Methodology, Conceptualization. Zhibing Wang: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Methodology. Shanshan Wei: Validation, Methodology.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Jilin Province (20220101350JC).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfa.2024.136037.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Label-free nanopore single-molecule measurement of trypsin activity

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Zhou, L. Wang, X. Chen, X. Guan, Label-free nanopore single-molecule measurement of trypsin activity, ACS Sens, 1 (2016) 607-613.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Proteomics using protease alternatives to trypsin benefits from sequential digestion with trypsin

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T. Dau, G. Bartolomucci, J. Rappsilber, Proteomics using protease alternatives to trypsin benefits from sequential digestion with trypsin, Anal Chem, 92 (2020) 9523-9527.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Efficient protein digestion using highly-stable and reproducible trypsin coatings on magnetic nanofibers

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B. Lee, B.C. Kim, M.S. Chang, H.S. Kim, H.B. Na, Y.I. Park, J. Lee, T. Hyeon, H. Lee, S.-W. Lee, J. Kim, Efficient protein digestion using highly-stable and reproducible trypsin coatings on magnetic nanofibers, Chemical Engineering Journal, 288 (2016) 770-777.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The study on the role of inflammatory cells and mediators in post-infectious functional dyspepsia

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Li, H. Chen, H. Lu, W. Li, X. Chen, Y. Peng, Z. Ge, The study on the role of inflammatory cells and mediators in post-infectious functional dyspepsia, Scand J Gastroenterol, 45 (2010) 573-581.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Genetic risk in chronic pancreatitis: the trypsin-dependent pathway

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: E. Hegyi, M. Sahin-Toth, Genetic risk in chronic pancreatitis: the trypsin-dependent pathway, Dig Dis Sci, 62 (2017) 1692-1701.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A fluorometric sensing method for sensitive detection of trypsin and its inhibitor based on gold nanoclusters and gold nanoparticles

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Wang, D. Su, G. Wang, X. Su, A fluorometric sensing method for sensitive detection of trypsin and its inhibitor based on gold nanoclusters and gold nanoparticles, Anal Bioanal Chem, 410 (2018) 6891-6900.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: An ultrasensitive fluorescent nanosensor for trypsin based on upconversion nanoparticles
